content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 607 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

October 4, 1976

Assemblyman Joseph Montoya
State Capitol Building, Room 4098
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Assemblyman Montoya:

Thank you for your letter of September 15, 1976,
requesting information concerning the applicability of various provisions of the Political Reform Act.

It should be emphasized at the outset that the
jurisdiction of the Commission is limited to the Political
Reform Act; and, accordingly, we intimate no opinion with
respect to whether the common law or other provisions of
California law would be applicable to the activities in
question. With respect to the Political Reform Act, none of
the activities described in your letter are prohibited.
Specifically, legislators and members of their staffs may
make payments in support of a lobbying organization, and
nothing in the Act restricts the amount or type of payments
which may be made for the purpose of lobbying. However, you
should be aware that if a person or a group of persons acting in concert makes payments of $250 or more in a month for
the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative
action, they will be required to file statements pursuant to
Gov. Code Section 86108(b).

Legislators and their staff members also may participate in the making of contributions as members of a lobbying
organization's board of directors. However, pursuant to
Gov. Code Sections 86200 and 86202 the organization's lobbyist
may not make or participate in the making, or arrange for
the making of any contributions.

I hope the foregoing is helpful. If you have any
specific questions with respect to how your organization
should report its lobbying or political activities, you may
wish to contact Nan Hambleton or Gina Kutchins of the Commission's
Technical Assistance Division.

Sincerely,

Michael Bennett
Executive Director

AGRICULTURE
ASSEMBLY RULES
EDUCATION

SELECT COMMITTEES
CHAIRMAN, IMPLEMENTATION OF
CAREER EDUCATION

JOSEPH B. MONTOYA

MEMBER
CALIFORNIA
ADVISORY COUNCIL
ON VOCATIONAL
EDUCATION AND
TECHNICAL TRAINING
STATE SCHOOLS AND
SERVICES ADVISORY BOARD

ASSEMBLYMAN, SIXTIETH DISTRICT

BASSETT, BALDWIN PARK, CITY OF INDUSTRY, EL MONTE,
LA PUENTE, NORTH WHITTIER, ROSEMEAD,
SOUTH EL MONTE, SOUTH SAN GABRIEL,
WEST COVINA, VALINDA

MAJORITY WHIP

September 15, 1976

Mr. Daniel H. Lowenstein, Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
925 "J" Street, Suite 522
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Lowenstein:

     This is to request information on the legality of undertaking several actions on behalf of a statewide lobbying organization that is in the process of being established. To my understanding, the lobbying organization will be nonprofit, will have a social welfare purpose primarily, and is considering making political campaign contributions at a later date.

Following are the questions requiring attention:

(1) Are members of the Legislature and legislative staff permitted to contribute to lobbying organizations?

If permitted:

     (a) What types of contributions are allowed, e.g., personal monetary gifts, funds raised by political campaign activities, or funds raised from the holding of special fund raisers for the express purpose of helping to finance a lobbying organization?

     (b) How much of a contribution is allowed?

     (c) What, if any, are the reporting requirements?

-2-

(2) Are members of the Legislature and legislative staff
permitted to participate in the decision making process of lobbying organizations, e.g., permitted to
act as a member of a board of directors to such an
organization?

(a) If the lobbying organization were to decide
to make political contributions (in legislative races), would members of the Legislature
and legislative staff be permitted to participate in the decision making process?

Your assistance in responding to this request will be
appreciated.

Sincerely,

JOSEPH B. MONTOYA

sections.conclusion: None of the activities described are prohibited under the Political Reform Act. Legislators and staff may make payments in support of lobbying organizations without restrictions on amount or type. They may also participate as members of a lobbying organization's board of directors. However, payments of $250 or more in a month for influencing legislative or administrative action trigger reporting requirements under Gov. Code Section 3610(b), and pursuant to Gov. Code Sections 86200 and 86202, the organization's lobbyist may not make or participate in the making of contributions.
sections.question_synthetic: The requester asks whether California legislators and legislative staff members may make contributions to and participate in the governance of lobbying organizations, and whether they may participate in decisions regarding political campaign contributions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that legislators and staff may make contributions to lobbying organizations and serve on their boards without violating the Political Reform Act, but contributions of $250+ monthly for lobbying require disclosure filings, and lobbyists themselves are prohibited from making or participating in political contributions.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document is an FPPC response letter (October 4, 1976) to an incoming request (September 15, 1975). Includes both the original request from Assemblyman Montoya and the FPPC's response. OCR quality affects confidence slightly; some text formatting issues noted but key sections clearly extractable.

CONCLUSION: None of the activities described are prohibited under the Political Reform Act. Legislators and staff may make payments in support of lobbying organizations without restrictions on amount or type. They may also participate as members of a lobbying organization's board of directors. However, payments of $250 or more in a month for influencing legislative or administrative action trigger reporting requirements under Gov. Code Section 3610(b), and pursuant to Gov. Code Sections 86200 and 86202, the organization's lobbyist may not make or participate in the making of contributions.

embedding.summary: FPPC responds to Assemblyman Montoya's inquiry about whether legislators and legislative staff may contribute to and participate in governing a nonprofit lobbying organization. The Commission advises that such contributions and board participation are permitted, subject to disclosure requirements for amounts over $250 monthly and restrictions on direct lobbyist participation in campaign contributions.
